sections.title: Part I Listening Comprehension - Section A
sections.instructions: In this part, you will hear 10 short conversations. At the end of each conversation, a question will be asked about what is said. Each conversation and the question will be spoken only once. When you hear the question, read the four choices of the answer given and choose the best one by marking the corresponding letter A, B, C, or D on your Answer Sheet I.

options.label: C

sections.title: Part I Listening Comprehension - Section B
sections.instructions: In this part, you will hear two mini-talks. While you listen, complete the sentences in your Answer Sheet II for Questions 11 to 20 by writing NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS in each sentence. You will hear each talk or conversation TWICE.Questions I1 to 15 are based on a talk about home security. You now have 30 seconds to read Questions 11 to 15. Questions 16 to 20 are based on a talk about eclipses. You now have 30 seconds to read Questions 16 to 20.

questions.text: Unlocked doors or windows leave easy _______ the home for burglars.
If your exterior doors have glass in them, the burglar can _______ and open the door.
An unsecured _______ gives a burglar an opportunity to enter your property or find tools to assist him.
One advice to avoid burglary is to keep the main entryways and exterior sections of your home lit _______
Overgrown bushes and trees which are close to your home provide leverage to _______
Looking up, you catch a _______ of what looks like a disk of pure blackness sliding across the face of the sun.
The Greek origin of the word eclipse is _______
A common embodiment of the eclipse was a _______ or demon.
While the ancient Chinese made great noise and commotion to _______ the dragon, the Incas tried to intimidate the sun-eating creatures.
According to David Dearborn, for most early cultures, the sun was seen as a _______

sections.title: Part I Listening Comprehension - Section C
sections.instructions: In this part, you will hear three mini-talks and each of them will be spoken only once. While listening to them, read the questions that follow each talk. At the end of each mini-talk you will hear the questions read to you. There will be a 40-second-pause after each question. During the pause, you will be asked to write down your answer on your Answer Sheet II, using one sentence only, either complete or incomplete. Your answer should be concise and to the point.Questions 21 to 23 are based on Mini-talk One: Mini-talk One Questions 24 to 26 are based on Mini-talk Two: Mini-talk Two Questions 27 to 30 are based on Mini-talk Three: Mini-talk Three

questions.text: What was considered impolite behavior for a man a few years ago?
questions.correctAnswer: To smoke on the street (or: when a lady was in the room).

questions.text: What should one do if a person cannot show up on time for a dinner invitation in the US?
questions.correctAnswer: He/She should call up to explain his/her delay.

questions.text: What is the important thing to remember about social customs?
questions.correctAnswer: To avoid making other people feel uncomfortable.

questions.text: What did ancient people believe lightning and thunder to be?
What is the function of lightning rods?
questions.correctAnswer: To protect buildings from damage by lightning.

questions.text: What is the safest place during a storm of lightning according to this talk?
What were put in the wallets before they were turned in to the police officers?
questions.correctAnswer: Cash, identification with names, addresses and phone numbers.

questions.text: How many wallets were recovered in 1970s in Miami?
What happened to some officers in Miami after that report?
questions.correctAnswer: They were fired or took early retirement.

questions.text: What was the result of the wallet test in LA?
questions.correctAnswer: All the 20 wallets were returned and not a penny was missing.

sections.title: Part II Use of English - Section A
sections.instructions: There are 15 blanks in the following passage. Read the passage carefully and fill in each of the blanks by choosing the right word or phrase from the list given below. Write your answer on the Answer Sheet II. Capitalize the word when it is necessary. The words and phrases listed are twice as many as the blanks. Once a word or phrase is chosen, it must be used only once.

questions.text: Fill in blank 31.
Fill in blank 32.
Fill in blank 33.
Fill in blank 34.
Fill in blank 35.
Fill in blank 36.
Fill in blank 37.
Fill in blank 38.
Fill in blank 39.
Fill in blank 40.
Fill in blank 41.
Fill in blank 42.
Fill in blank 43.
Fill in blank 44.
Fill in blank 45.

sections.content:
My major social concerns all revolve around the future. In other words, I'm concerned about what prevents people from rising above their disadvantages. {{31}} I am most concerned with the handicapped, education, and crime.

I feel society's response to handicaps is {{32}} really hampers the potential of the disabled. It is important for the disabled to get a better {{33}} of worth and to be able to adapt to, and survive in, today's world. Through National Honor Society (NHS), I have done just that. I have helped out at a lock-in that was designed to foster {{34}} among the children of the organization, as well as at Special Olympics, {{35}} the children participate in sports on a competitive basis so their talents and abilities can be {{36}} Whenever the disabled can be successful at an activity, the {{37}} between them and the rest of society is drastically reduced.

Education is the key to other problems such as gangs, drugs, and crime {{38}} it can prevent and eliminate them. I try to get students in our school to {{39}} their opportunities by using the educational resources {{40}} By setting up a tutoring program through NHS, I have matched up needy students with other students who can assist them with their problems in classes. More directly, I help students out with English and show them how to use the Writing Center Lab, a(n) {{41}} for English students at any level. The more educated a person is, I believe, the more able he is to be successful in the future.

I have {{42}} criminal problems in my school by discussing solutions to gangs and other crime in the Student Advisory Committee. We have drafted several {{43}} to help reduce those problems in our school.

Educating people about such social concerns is also very crucial because they won't fix what they don't think is broken. That is one {{44}} of our newspaper, in which we have written various editorials and news stories to educate the student body on social topics. Through debate, I myself have become {{45}} on such topics as the homeless, poverty, health care, and the environment. That way I can practice vihat 1 preach.
sections.passageAnalysis:
### 1. 文章大意
本文作者阐述了自己最关心的三大社会问题：残疾人权益、教育和犯罪。作者认为，社会对残疾人的态度限制了他们的潜力，而教育是解决帮派、毒品和犯罪等问题的关键。文章通过分享作者在“国家荣誉协会”等平台上的具体实践，如帮助残疾儿童、辅导学生学业、参与校园犯罪问题讨论等，论证了通过教育和社会参与来应对这些社会问题的可行性与重要性。

### 2. 语篇结构
- **第一段（引言）**：开门见山，点明作者关注的是影响人们摆脱困境的社会问题，并具体列出三大核心关切：残疾人、教育和犯罪。
- **第二段（论述残疾人问题）**：分析社会对残疾人的态度是其发展的主要障碍，并举例说明通过活动促进互动与认可，可以打破社会隔阂。
- **第三段（论述教育问题）**：提出教育是解决犯罪等问题的根本途径，并详细介绍了通过辅导项目、利用写作中心等具体行动来帮助学生最大化利用教育资源。
- **第四段（论述犯罪问题）**：简要说明通过学生咨询委员会讨论和起草提案来应对校园犯罪问题。
- **第五段（总结与延伸）**：强调教育公众的重要性，并以校报和辩论活动为例，说明如何提升对社会议题的认识并付诸实践。

### 3. 核心词汇
1.  **concerns** （名词）关切，关注的问题
2.  **handicapped / disabled** （名词/形容词）残疾人/残疾的
3.  **hampers** （动词）阻碍，妨碍
4.  **interaction** （名词）互动，交流
5.  **recognized** （动词）被认可，被承认
6.  **barrier** （名词）障碍，隔阂
7.  **maximize** （动词）最大化，充分利用
8.  **available** （形容词）可用的，可获得的
9.  **indispensable** （形容词/名词）不可或缺的（事物）
10. **dealt with** （动词短语）处理，应对
11. **proposals** （名词）提案，建议
12. **objective** （名词）目标，目的
13. **knowledgeable** （形容词）知识渊博的，熟悉的
14. **eliminate** （动词）消除，根除
15. **crucial** （形容词）至关重要的，关键的

sections.title: Part II Reading Comprehension - Section B - Passage 1
sections.instructions: Read the following passages carefully and then select the best answer from among the four choices given to answer each of the questions or complete each of the statements that follow each passage. Mark the letter of your choice on your Answer Sheet I.

questions.text: The author holds that politics of democracy of the 19th century

options.label: A
options.text: allowed people to have greater say than nowadays.

options.label: B
options.text: was more advanced than that of Iraq.

options.label: C
options.text: put females at a disadvantage.

options.label: D
options.text: reached its peak of development.

questions.text: It can be safely said that men outdo women

options.label: A
options.text: superficially.

options.label: B
options.text: instinctively.

options.label: C
options.text: physically.

options.label: D
options.text: emotionally.

questions.text: What is discussed as on disadvantage for women to become leaders?

options.label: A
options.text: They tend to complain a lot.

options.label: B
options.text: They often shed tears and shout.

options.label: C
options.text: They have a weak will.

options.label: D
options.text: They lack military experience.

questions.text: What is implied about the Bush Administration?

options.label: A
options.text: They incorrectly call the terrorists “uncivilized”.

options.label: B
options.text: Its policy is just as primitive.

options.label: C
options.text: It found a proper excuse to invade Iraq.

options.label: D
options.text: It should assimilate other civilizations.

questions.text: What is the main idea of the last paragraph?

options.label: A
options.text: Comparison and contrast of brutal and inhumane.

options.label: B
options.text: Definition of primitive politics.

options.label: C
options.text: Illustration of brutality and atrocity.

options.label: D
options.text: Historical development of inhumane human behavior.

sections.content:
Although there are clear exceptions, in general it is males who get to choose. This translates into the American politics of the 19th century — males get to vote; females do not. This clearly was a more primitive time in the politics of democracy. Without debating how far we have advanced in the U.S., this issue is still being fought in the cities and towns of Afghanistan, Iraq, Iran, and other countries in the Islamic world and elsewhere.

In a modern age, it is difficult to maintain that men have anything other than size and strength as an advantage over women. Yet, we continue to argue — openly or implicitly — that men should be leaders and women should not. Look at the upcoming 2004 presidential election. Both parties have played up the roles of military experience and leadership as critical factors in potential political chiefdom. It is the same as saying that political leaders have to be warriors. This certainly would rule out the leadership skills of women, few of whom serve in combat roles let alone have opportunities to lead a screaming charge. Biology determines the nature of political rule.

Primitive is the not the same as "uncivilized," at least not in the way the latter word has been used by the Bush Administration in labeling terrorists. War is war and there is nothing more primitive in the deeds of the terrorists than in those of the U.S. armed forces. Uncivilized in this usage really just means "not from our civilization." It does not signify primitive, although it certainly is implied.

The word also is sometimes incorrectly used to mean brutal or inhumane. Here we have to be cognizant of writer Mark Twain's observation that those acts we label as “inhumane" are really only those behaviors conducted by humans, so it is a misnomer at best. Among humans, brutality is something that we want to think of as reflecting human behavior of an earlier time. Torture, rape, genocide, and use of insidious weapons are methods that we want to associate with ruthless Mongolian warlord Genghis Khan. Then we have Rwanda, Serbia, Sierra Leone, Myanmar, Sudan, Cambodia under Pol Pot, the Congo, and numerous others (some specifically unnamed) where each of these behaviors has been ruthlessly employed. Were we being primitives just last year or last month?

sections.title: Part II Reading Comprehension - Section B - Passage 2
sections.instructions: Read the following passages carefully and then select the best answer from among the four choices given to answer each of the questions or complete each of the statements that follow each passage. Mark the letter of your choice on your Answer Sheet I.

questions.text: What is the common image of nature?

options.label: A
options.text: It is full of danger.

options.label: B
options.text: It brims with troubling bugs and infections.

options.label: C
options.text: It seems to provide beauty and romance.

options.label: D
options.text: It evokes boredom in people.

questions.text: It is implied in the first two paragraph that people

options.label: A
options.text: are very liable to get into trouble in nature.

options.label: B
options.text: have wrong ideas about nature.

options.label: C
options.text: are likely to feel real nature if careful enough.

options.label: D
options.text: need protection when go to nature.

questions.text: What does the sentence "it is uninformed talk” in the second paragraph probably mean?

options.label: A
options.text: People only imagine what nature is but they do not know what it is exactly.

options.label: B
options.text: As more and more people are moving to cities, they enjoy going to nature.

options.label: C
options.text: Nature is the symbol of simplicity that does not require much information.

options.label: D
options.text: Taking a relaxing trip to nature serves as a trigger of endless talk in cities.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of the third paragraph?

options.label: A
options.text: To list the cases where people die accidentally.

options.label: B
options.text: To show the connection between carelessness and danger.

options.label: C
options.text: To trace the cause of some people's peril.

options.label: D
options.text: To illustrate people's ignorance of real nature.

questions.text: According to the last paragraph,

options.label: A
options.text: people often curse nature because it is not as expected.

options.label: B
options.text: nature is accurately represented as it is on TV.

options.label: C
options.text: tough guys manage to change nature.

options.label: D
options.text: people are shocked to find that nature is beyond man's control.

sections.content:
If you put yourself in nature, if only for a matter of days, you quickly will be disabused of all your romantic fantasies. Take a trek through the jungles of Borneo, and in short order you will have festering sores on your skin and bugs all over your body, biting in your hair and crawling up your nose and into your ears. You will have infections and sickness, and if you are not with somebody who knows what he or she is doing, you very rapidly will starve to death. Chances are that even in the jungles of Borneo you will not experience nature so directly since you will have covered your entire body with DEET.

The truth is, almost nobody wants to experience real nature. What people desire is to spend a week or two in a cabin in the woods, with screens on the windows. They want a simplified life for a while, a nice river rafting trip for a few days, with somebody else doing the cooking. Nobody wants to go back to nature in any real sense, and no one does. It is all talk, and as the years go on and the world population grows increasingly urban, it is uninformed talk. Farmers know of what they speak; city people do not. They just their fantasies.

One way to measure the prevalence of fantasy is to note the number of people who die because they have not the least knowledge of how nature truly is. They stand beside wild animals, like buffalo, for a picture and get trampled; they climb a mountain in dangerous weather, without the proper gear, and freeze to death. They drown in the surf on their holiday because they cannot conceive the real power of what we blithely call "the force of nature." They have seen the ocean, but they never have been in it.

The television generation expects nature to act the way they picture it. They think all life experiences can be TiVo-ed. The notion that the natural world obeys its own rules and does not give a damn about their expectations comes as a massive shock. Well-to-do, educated individuals in an urban environment experience the ability to fashion their daily lives as they wish. They buy clothes that suit their taste and decorate their apartments as they like. Within limits, they can contrive a daily urban world that pleases them. The natural world is not so malleable, however. On the contrary, it will demand that you adapt to it and if you do not, you will die. It is a harsh, powerful, and unforgiving world that most urban Westerners never have experienced.

sections.title: Part II Reading Comprehension - Section B - Passage 3
sections.instructions: Read the following passages carefully and then select the best answer from among the four choices given to answer each of the questions or complete each of the statements that follow each passage. Mark the letter of your choice on your Answer Sheet I.

questions.text: What is looked down upon in the US?

options.label: A
options.text: To obtain relief to plant crops.

options.label: B
options.text: To use government relief in consumption.

options.label: C
options.text: To change policies frequently.

options.label: D
options.text: To depend on the government for money all the time.

questions.text: Aid to families with dependent children and that to American farmers are similar in that

options.label: A
options.text: they both encourage self-sufficiency.

options.label: B
options.text: they both facilitate cooperation between generations.

options.label: C
options.text: they both discourage hard working.

options.label: D
options.text: they both are unanimously approved of.

questions.text: The problem that Lyle Sjostrom is likely to encounter is that

options.label: A
options.text: equipment and fertilizer would impose a burden on his life.

options.label: B
options.text: his son has to become a farmer in his spare time.

options.label: C
options.text: he has to allow part of his land to be at the mercy of the government.

options.label: D
options.text: he was likely to be accused of cheating.

questions.text: What does the sentence "working a farm and relying on subsidies has been a plus for me" mean?

options.label: A
options.text: Depending on the government is humiliating.

options.label: B
options.text: Working a farm and depending on government support benefits me a lot.

options.label: C
options.text: Being a farmer has become a burden for me.

options.label: D
options.text: Working a farm requires me to put in extra work and money.

questions.text: What is the main idea of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: Government money to support farmers is not always effectively made use of.

options.label: B
options.text: American farmers have to do with the rise and fall of economy.

options.label: C
options.text: The government does not offer good care to cover American farmers.

options.label: D
options.text: American farmers are clever enough to take advantage of the government.

sections.content:
Why is there a stigma attached to using government-financed stamps to purchase food but no stigma attached to accepting government money to grow the food in the first place? American farm policy is filled with such stumpers.

Consider that federal cash payments to individuals — the program known as Aid to Families with Dependent Children — were widely criticized for creating intergenerational dependency on the federal government and allowing people to maintain an idle lifestylc. Yct cash payments to American farmers are justified by some precisely because they promote intergenerational dependency on government and allow for an idle lifestyle.

"If we'd kept our land in wheat, the fixed costs for equipment and fertilizer would have played havoc with our budget," North Dakota farmer Lyle Sjostrom told The New York Times in late August. He dedicated 40 percent of his 4,000 acres to the federal government's conservation prograın, a move he credits with allowing his son, a full-time seed peddler, to become a farmer in his spare time. (When women on welfare work a job on the side to supplement their government checks it's considered welfare fraud, but when farmers take outside jobs, such as selling seeds or legislating farm policy, it's considered noble.)

Others praise farm handouts for the leisure they provide. "Subsidies are critical to most gentlemen farming in Arkansas,” Sen. Blanche Lincoln (D-Ark.) told the Times.

Lincoln is one of seven congressional farmers who receive thousands of dollars every year from taxpayers. Some, like Rep. Charles Stenholm (D-Texas), an author of farm legislation, find value in dependence on government. "Working a farm and relying on subsidies has been a plus for me," Stenholm told the Times. Perhaps that's why Stenholm joined with Rep. Marion Berry (D-Ark.) in voting against the modest tax cut passed earlier this year. They recognized that handing people's money back in individual increments of up to $300 means there is less money for five- and six-figure checks to farmers such as themselves. (Berry's family enterprises raked in $649,750 in farm subsidies over the last five years. Stenholm received $39,298 over tne same period.)

Advocates for American farmers are extremely worried about what the declining fortune of the federal treasury means for them. In July, Democrats were forced to accept $2 billion less in the routine emergency farm bill (the end result: farmers only got a bonus $5.5 billion in their pockets this time around). And, as of press time, the House was set to debate a 10-year, $171 billion farm package that guarantees payments to indigent farmers.

options.text: As a result, staple foods cooked in water are normally fed to weaning age children in the form of gruels.

options.label: C
options.text: The practice has been a traditional way of food preservation in many parts of the world.

options.label: D
options.text: Safer weaning can be achieved through preparation of hygienic and nutritionally balanced weaning foods with high energy density per unit volume, of which enough can be eaten.

options.label: E
options.text: Nutrition and health promotion programs can benefit greatly from such strategies in order to reduce the prevalence of food-related diarrhoeal diseases, as well as to increase energy intakes of many poor weaning-age children around the world.

options.label: F
options.text: "Dialogue on Diarrhoea" has recently produced an issue in which the method of fermentation is discussed for a safe weaning.

questions.text: Fill in blank 65.

options.label: A
options.text: Access to inadequate facilities — for both preparation and storage of foods — by many poor households contribute substantially to weaning food contamination.

options.label: B
options.text: As a result, staple foods cooked in water are normally fed to weaning age children in the form of gruels.

options.label: C
options.text: The practice has been a traditional way of food preservation in many parts of the world.

options.label: D
options.text: Safer weaning can be achieved through preparation of hygienic and nutritionally balanced weaning foods with high energy density per unit volume, of which enough can be eaten.

options.label: E
options.text: Nutrition and health promotion programs can benefit greatly from such strategies in order to reduce the prevalence of food-related diarrhoeal diseases, as well as to increase energy intakes of many poor weaning-age children around the world.

options.label: F
options.text: "Dialogue on Diarrhoea" has recently produced an issue in which the method of fermentation is discussed for a safe weaning.

sections.title: Part III Writing - Section A (Summary Writing)
sections.instructions: Read the following article and write a summary of no more than 150 words on your Answer Sheet II.

questions.text: Write a summary of the article above, no more than 150 words.
questions.correctAnswer: A doctor's function has changed over time. In the past, the doctor was a person who treated the diseases. Now a doctor is a stranger who combats diseases. They are expected not only to prevent death but also to improve their patient's quality of life. Many times there is nothing a doctor can do to prevent a patient from dying if the patient has a terminal disease; all he/she can do is waiting for death to arrive. This waiting time can be very painful for both the patients and the people who surround them. Not practicing euthanasia at the request of the dying person is violating a person's rights, creating an economic burden and increasing suffering. First of all, deciding to be alive or not is a personal decision. Neither the doctors nor the government has the power to decide if you should live or not. Since it is not their life and you are not in your situation, they cannot make that kind of decision for you. They give us the liberty to decide our job, our family, our religion, and even our sex preference. Why should they not give us the right to decide if we want to live or not? Next, the economic factor should be considered. Many times we are just wasting time and money on a situation that won't get better. If the patient wants euthanasia, why not do it and end the huge amount of money wasted on a treatment that won't help.. Finally, the basic reason why euthanasia should be practiced is because it stops the person from suffering. Having a patient suffering is not giving him a better quality of life. When the patient feels he is not getting the quality of life he wants, the least a doctor can do is grant him what he wants. If the patient's decision is to die, so do it. Why not end this suffering if we have the power? There is no point waiting for the person to die if there is nothing the doctors can do about it. If the person wishes to die, we should satisfy his last wish. This is why euthanasia should be legal for anyone who desires it.
questions.explanation:
### 1. 参考范文 (Model Summary)
Americans highly value a white smile but neglect dental care, with nearly 60% lacking a regular dentist. This neglect has led to widespread oral diseases, which are now understood as gateways to serious systemic conditions like heart disease, diabetes, and respiratory illnesses. Fortunately, modern dentistry has become far more comfortable and less painful, utilizing advanced technology for precise, minimally invasive procedures that often improve both dental health and cosmetic appearance. For instance, early cavity detection allows for small fillings that preserve tooth strength, while restorative work can correct wear and alignment issues, enhancing both function and aesthetics—a concept termed "functional aesthetics." The most common issue, periodontal disease, begins as reversible gingivitis but can trigger harmful chain reactions if untreated. Oral cancer, another major threat, often goes undetected until advanced stages, underscoring the need for regular self-exams and professional checkups. Ultimately, maintaining oral health is straightforward: consistent brushing, flossing, and biannual dental visits can prevent severe diseases and avoid costly future treatments.

### 2. 摘要逻辑 (Summary Logic)
摘要首先点明核心矛盾：美国人重视牙齿美观却疏于护理，并引用数据（近60%无牙医）佐证。接着，逻辑链条转向忽视口腔健康的严重后果，即口腔疾病作为“门户”会引发全身性慢性病。然后，转折至积极面：现代牙科技术的进步使治疗更舒适、精准且兼具美容效果，并举“功能性美学”为例。之后，重点阐述了两种主要风险：牙周病（从牙龈炎发展为系统性疾病）和口腔癌（强调早期检测的重要性）。最后，总结出简单、低成本的口腔卫生习惯（刷牙、用牙线、定期检查）是预防关键。这样构建确保了摘要覆盖了问题的严重性、解决方案和预防措施，逻辑连贯。

### 3. 重点表达 (Key Expressions)
1.  **are now understood as gateways to** - 现被视为引发...的门户
2.  **Fortunately, ... has become far more** - 幸运的是，...已变得更为...
3.  **For instance, ... allows for** - 例如，...使得...成为可能
4.  **Ultimately, ... is straightforward** - 最终，...是简单直接的
5.  **can prevent ... and avoid** - 能够预防...并避免...

sections.content:
Americans rank a bright, white smile as one of the most important aspects of an attractive appearance. Yet adults in our country get a barely passing grade when it comes to taking care of their teeth. According to the Academy of General Dentistry, almost 60 percent of Americans don't even have a dentist.

Our poor oral health report card means that diseases that start in the mouth have reached epidemic proportions, according to a report released by former surgeon general David Satcher, M.D. "We're just beginning to understand how the mouth serves as a gateway to infections that can trigger a host of chronic diseases," says Gordon L. Douglass, D.D.S., president of the American Academy of Periodontology (AAP). That's why visiting a dentist regularly can be a lifesaver.

Excuses for neglecting oral health are predictable enough — people fear the pain, the cost and the scary diagnosis they might hear precisely because they've stayed away for so long. Here's some good news to entice them back: “The dentist's office is no longer a torture chamber," says Richard Hansen, D.M.D., whose practice is in Fullerton, California. "We're investing in comfortable, heated cushions for the chairs and virtual-reality headsets, for example." Dr. Hansen and a growing number of dentists are also setting aside the drill in favor of technology that eliminates almost entirely the pain and the shots associated with filling cavities and other common procedures. And finally, "Some of the therapeutic procedures used today have a huge cosmetic bonus," says New York City's Jonathan Levine, D.M.D., a specialist in reconstructive and cosmetic dentistry. "They can actually make your teeth look better."

"Finding cavities sooner allows us to use pinpoint fillings," says Dr. Hansen. He explains that removing and replacing small areas of decay is far preferable to creating a huge hole in the tooth's core to clear away a more advanced cavity — a process that compromises the strength and longevity of the tooth. Some of the most significant advancements in dentistry have affected restoration, repair and replacement work — which now result in teeth being stronger, healthier and also more attractive. Dr. Levine calls this marriage of health and good looks "functional aesthetics." For example, he explains, "If the enamel on several of your teeth has eroded from wear, which can compromise their health, we can repair that damage with certain substances. At the same time, we can reshape and realign those teeth, if necessary, to make your smile more cosmetically appealing, as well as correct how the teeth come together. Often the proper realignment and reshaping of teeth will prevent the same wear from happening again."

Periodontal disorders, which affect a third of America's adult population, start mildly enough, often with gingivitis, an easily reversible condition caused by bacteria-laden plaque, with symptoms that include swollen, tender and bleeding gums. Mounting research suggests that gingivitis, if left untreated, spreads to threaten much more than the mouth: Full-blown periodontal disease can contribute to the blood clotting that causes heart attacks and strokes; it's also linked to the onset of type 2 diabetes, chronic bronchitis and, among pregnant women, premature delivery.

In the case of diabetes, the chain reaction seems to start with bacteria entering the bloodstream, where they cause immune cells to produce high levels of substances that can damage the cells responsible for insulin production, according to new research. When it comes to heart disease, bacteria and their byproducts cause inflammation in the arteries, which may contribute to blockages.

And with chronic bronchitis and emphysema, research suggests that bacteria in the mouth travel via inhalation to lodge in lung tissue, making the existing diseases worse. People with family histories of these diseases are at the highest risk — but only after the underlying periodontal disorder is full-blown are the chain reactions triggered.

Oral cancer, the sixth most common cancer worldwide, is life threatening, too. To aid in early detection, the ADA has launched a campaign to encourage adults to examine themselves for suspicious lumps and lesions. Currently, because these cancers are usually caught after they've spread to the lymph nodes in the neck, for example — only about half of all oral-cancer sufferers survive for more than five years after diagnosis. If your dentist doesn't initiate a checkup, insist on one. And it's a misperception that all oral cancers are caused by cigarettes, according to Sol Silverman, D.D.S., a spokesperson for the ADA. A full 25 percent of oral cancers strike nonsmokers, he says.

The bottom line, according to the AAP: Three easy oralhealth habits — twice-daily brushing, daily flossing and twice-annual dental checkups, including tooth cleaning — stop gingivitis before it can progress. Establishing these routine and low-cost habits can eliminate the need for the enormously expensive restorative work that's typically required later in life if oral health has been neglected.

sections.title: Part III Writing - Section B (Essay Writing)
sections.instructions: Write an essay of no less than 250 words on the topic given below. Use the proper space on your Answer Sheet II.

questions.text: Topic: There's a hot debate on legalizing assisted suicide and euthanasia. Please state your opinion on it with sounding reasons.
questions.correctAnswer: A doctor's function has changed over time. In the past, the doctor was a person who treated the diseases. Now a doctor is a stranger who combats diseases. They are expected not only to prevent death but also to improve their patient's quality of life. Many times there is nothing a doctor can do to prevent a patient from dying if the patient has a terminal disease; all he/she can do is waiting for death to arrive. This waiting time can be very painful for both the patients and the people who surround them. Not practicing euthanasia at the request of the dying person is violating a person's rights, creating an economic burden and increasing suffering. First of all, deciding to be alive or not is a personal decision. Neither the doctors nor the government has the power to decide if you should live or not. Since it is not their life and you are not in your situation, they cannot make that kind of decision for you. They give us the liberty to decide our job, our family, our religion, and even our sex preference. Why should they not give us the right to decide if we want to live or not? Next, the economic factor should be considered. Many times we are just wasting time and money on a situation that won't get better. If the patient wants euthanasia, why not do it and end the huge amount of money wasted on a treatment that won't help.. Finally, the basic reason why euthanasia should be practiced is because it stops the person from suffering. Having a patient suffering is not giving him a better quality of life. When the patient feels he is not getting the quality of life he wants, the least a doctor can do is grant him what he wants. If the patient's decision is to die, so do it. Why not end this suffering if we have the power? There is no point waiting for the person to die if there is nothing the doctors can do about it. If the person wishes to die, we should satisfy his last wish. This is why euthanasia should be legal for anyone who desires it.
questions.explanation:
### 1. 参考范文 (Model Essay)

The question of whether to legalize assisted suicide and euthanasia is one of the most profound ethical dilemmas facing modern societies. While the sanctity of life is a cornerstone of medical ethics, I firmly believe that, under stringent legal safeguards, individuals suffering from terminal and incurable illnesses should have the right to a dignified and self-determined end. This position is grounded in the principles of personal autonomy, compassion, and the pragmatic realities of end-of-life care.

Firstly, the principle of personal autonomy is paramount. Adults of sound mind have the right to make fundamental decisions about their own bodies and lives. If a person is enduring unbearable and untreatable suffering with no prospect of improvement, denying them the choice to end their life humanely is a profound violation of their self-determination. It forces them to endure a prolonged existence that they deem devoid of meaning and quality, effectively prioritizing abstract notions of life over the individual's lived experience.

Secondly, legalization is an act of profound compassion. Modern medicine can often prolong biological life but fails to alleviate existential suffering. For patients in the advanced stages of debilitating diseases like motor neuron disease or certain cancers, the final months can be a harrowing ordeal of pain, loss of dignity, and total dependence. To offer these individuals a legal, peaceful, and medically supervised alternative is to acknowledge their suffering and respect their humanity. It allows for a controlled, good-farewell process with loved ones, rather than a traumatic, drawn-out decline.

Opponents often cite the "slippery slope" argument, fearing that legalization could lead to coercion or devalue vulnerable lives. However, these risks can be mitigated through rigorous regulatory frameworks, as demonstrated in jurisdictions like the Netherlands and Switzerland. Strict protocols involving multiple independent medical assessments, psychological evaluations, repeated patient consent, and mandatory waiting periods can effectively protect against abuse. The goal is not to encourage death but to provide a last-resort option for those for whom palliative care cannot suffice.

In conclusion, while the sanctity of life remains a vital principle, it must be balanced with the equally important values of autonomy and mercy. The legalization of assisted dying, within a tightly controlled legal framework, represents a mature and compassionate societal response to extreme human suffering. It empowers individuals with a final, fundamental choice and aligns medical practice with a holistic understanding of human dignity that encompasses not just the preservation of life, but the quality of its conclusion.

### 2. 写作思路 (Writing Strategy)

*   **文章结构 (Structure):** 采用经典的“观点-论证-结论”议论文结构。
    *   **引言段:** 直接切入主题，承认议题的复杂性，并清晰亮明个人立场（支持在有严格监管下合法化），同时概括核心论点（个人自主权、同情心、现实考量）。
    *   **主体段 1:** 论证第一个核心理由——**个人自主权**。阐述个人对自己生命和身体拥有决定权，拒绝此权利是对自主权的侵犯。
    *   **主体段 2:** 论证第二个核心理由——**同情与尊严**。从现实角度描述绝症患者的痛苦，指出合法化是人性化的体现，允许有尊严的告别。
    *   **主体段 3:（让步与反驳）** 主动提及并反驳主要反对观点（“滑坡效应”和滥用风险）。通过引用已立法的国家案例和提出具体监管措施（多重评估、等待期等）来有效化解反对意见，增强论证的全面性和说服力。
    *   **结论段:** 总结核心论点，重申在严格框架下合法化是平衡生命神圣、个人自主与慈悲的成熟做法，并升华到对“尊严”更全面的理解。

*   **论证手法 (Arguments):**
    *   **原则性论证:** 引用“个人自主权”这一普世伦理原则作为理论基础。
    *   **情感与事实结合论证:** 通过描述绝症患者的“痛苦”、“丧失尊严”等具体情境引发共情，同时以“现代医学能延长生命但无法消除痛苦”这一事实作为支撑。
    *   **反驳性论证:** 预判并系统性地反驳反方关键论点，展示思考的深度和辩证性。
    *   **例证法:** 提及荷兰、瑞士等实际案例，使论证更具现实依据。

*   **语言基调 (Tone):** 保持客观、理性、富有同理心且坚定。使用正式、学术化的词汇，避免情绪化表达。在讨论患者痛苦时语气庄重而充满同情，在反驳论点时语气逻辑严谨。

### 3. 亮点词汇与句型 (Key Vocabulary & Expressions)

1.  **Ethical dilemma** - 伦理困境
    *   *(用于描述此类议题的根本性质)*
2.  **Sanctity of life** - 生命的神圣性
    *   *(核心反对理由的关键概念)*
3.  **Personal autonomy / Self-determination** - 个人自主权 / 自我决定权
    *   *(支持方核心论点的基石词汇)*
4.  **A dignified end** - 有尊严的终结
    *   *(核心诉求的精准表达)*
5.  **Stringent legal safeguards / Rigorous regulatory framework** - 严格的法律保障 / 严谨的监管框架
    *   *(论证合法化可行性的关键条件)*
6.  **The slippery slope argument** - 滑坡效应论点
    *   *(指代“一旦开口就会导致更坏后果”的经典反对逻辑)*
7.  **Untreatable suffering / Existential suffering** - 无法治疗的痛苦 / 存在性痛苦
    *   *(区分生理痛苦与精神绝望的高级表达)*
8.  **Mitigate the risks** - 降低风险
    *   *(讨论如何应对潜在问题的实用动词短语)*
